primary.question: Under which circumstances can a concern or complaint be made under this University of Essex whistleblowing policy?
primary.expected_answer: A concern or complaint can be raised if it is in the public interest, i.e., it involves serious wrongdoing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure.
primary.actual_answer:
A concern or complaint can be made under the University of Essex whistleblowing policy when it is in the public interest, meaning it is a disclosure of serious wrongdoing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf). This policy applies to all employees, students, workers, contractors, subcontractors, suppliers, visitors, volunteers, and invitees at the University of Essex. However, concerns of a personal nature should be raised under the Code of Student Conduct or the Student Concerns and Complaints Procedure (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/student-concerns-complaints-procedure.pdf). 

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/student-concerns-complaints-procedure.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: Under which circumstances can a concern or complaint be made under this University of Essex whistleblowing policy?

judge.justification: The assistant's answer is fully correct and matches the reference answer's substance while also providing additional relevant details.

follow_up.question: What happens after a concern is disclosed under this policy?
follow_up.expected_answer: After a concern is disclosed, the individual will typically make their disclosure to the University Secretary, who may then initiate an investigation. The individual making the disclosure will be kept informed of progress.
follow_up.actual_answer:
After a concern is disclosed under this policy, the University Secretary (or an alternative set out in the policy) will acknowledge the disclosure in writing within 10 working days (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf). The next steps may include an investigation under the University's Conducting Investigations Guidance, an 'ad hoc' investigation, a referral to the police, or no further action if the concern is judged to be without merit (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf). The individual raising the concern will be kept informed of progress and the outcome, while maintaining confidentiality for other parties involved (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf).

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: What happens after a concern is disclosed under the University of Essex whistleblowing policy?

judge.justification: The assistant's answer is fully correct and complete, matching the reference answer's substance and providing additional details from the policy document.

primary.question: What constitutes plagiarism according to this document?
primary.expected_answer: Plagiarism involves using or copying someone else's work without proper acknowledgment in any assignment, examination, or other assessed work.
primary.actual_answer:
According to the provided documents, plagiarism is defined as using or copying someone else's work (whether written, printed, or in any other form) without proper acknowledgment in any assignment, examination, or other assessed work (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedure.pdf). This includes copying a paragraph from a book without citing the original author. 

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedure.pdf 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedures-2017-18.pdf

follow_up.actual_answer:
According to the document (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedure.pdf), an example of plagiarism is copying a paragraph from a book without citing the original author.

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedure.pdf

primary.question: What are some examples of concerns and complaints that could be raised under this procedure?
primary.expected_answer: Examples include failure by the University to meet its obligations, misleading information provided in prospectuses or promotional material, concerns about the delivery of a program, teaching or administration, and poor quality facilities, learning resources, or services provided directly by the University.
primary.actual_answer:
Some examples of concerns and complaints that could be raised under this procedure include: 
- failure by the University to meet its obligations, 
- misleading or incorrect information provided by the University, 
- concerns about the delivery of a programme, teaching or administration, 
- poor quality facilities, learning resources or services provided directly by the University (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/student-concerns-complaints-procedure.pdf).

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are some examples of concerns and complaints that could be raised under this procedure?

judge.justification: The assistant's answer is mostly correct but slightly imprecise in wording and lacks a direct reference to prospectuses or promotional material.

follow_up.question: What are the stages involved in raising a complaint under this procedure?
follow_up.expected_answer: The stages involve Early Resolution and Formal Complaint. Students are directed to the next stage if they remain dissatisfied at the conclusion of the Early Resolution stage.
follow_up.actual_answer:
The stages involved in raising a complaint under this procedure are: 
a. Early Resolution (see Section 4) - seeks to resolve concerns swiftly and effectively at the point at which the concern is raised, 
b. Formal Complaint (see Section 5) - is appropriate where a student is dissatisfied with the outcome of early resolution, 
c. Internal Review (see Section 7) - provides an opportunity for a formal Internal Review, 
d. External Review (see Section 8) - where the complainant remains dissatisfied, they can submit a request for a review by the Office of the Independent Adjudicator (OIA) 
(Sources: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/student-concerns-complaints-procedure.pdf)

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are the stages involved in raising a complaint under this procedure?

judge.justification: The assistant's answer includes additional stages not mentioned in the reference answer, but it is mostly correct and provides more detail.

primary.question: What are the core values that the University of Essex promotes regarding academic freedom and freedom of speech?
primary.expected_answer: Academic freedom and freedom of speech within the law are fundamental commitments at the heart of the mission at the University of Essex, which we promote as core values and vital components of being an inclusive community.
primary.actual_answer:
The University of Essex promotes academic freedom and freedom of speech within the law as core values and vital components of being an inclusive community (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf). 

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are the core values that the University of Essex promotes regarding academic freedom and freedom of speech?

judge.justification: The answer is mostly correct but lacks the direct reference to these values being 'fundamental commitments at the heart of the mission'.

follow_up.question: How does the University ensure that these values are upheld in practice?
follow_up.expected_answer: The University ensures these values are upheld by exercising freedoms with due regard to shared community values, promoting an enabling and inclusive environment which respects the rights of others.
follow_up.actual_answer:
The University of Essex ensures that the values of academic freedom and freedom of speech are upheld in practice by having a Code of Practice: Academic Freedom and Freedom of Speech (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf). This code outlines the University's commitment to promoting an environment where intense inquiry and informed argument can generate lasting ideas, and where academic staff have freedom within the law to question and test received wisdom, and to put forward new ideas and controversial or unpopular opinions.

The University also ensures that access to its premises is not denied to any member of the University or invited external speaker on grounds relating to their ideas or opinions, unless the expression of such ideas is unlawful (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf).

Additionally, the University recognizes that counter-speech and protest are characteristics of a university committed to academic freedom, freedom of speech, and inclusion, but emphasizes that the right to protest should never prevent others from exercising their right to freedom of speech within the law (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf).

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: How does the University of Essex ensure that academic freedom and freedom of speech are upheld in practice?

judge.justification: The answer is comprehensive but focuses more on academic freedom and speech than the broader community values mentioned in the reference.

primary.question: What are the fundamental commitments that the University of Essex promotes as core values under this Code of Practice?
primary.expected_answer: Academic freedom and freedom of speech within the law are fundamental commitments at the heart of the mission at the University of Essex.
primary.actual_answer:
The University of Essex promotes Academic Freedom and Freedom of Speech within the law as fundamental commitments at the heart of its mission, which are core values and vital components of being an inclusive community (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf). 

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf

follow_up.question: How does the University ensure that these core values are promoted in academic and university life?
follow_up.expected_answer: Promoting the lawful expression of diverse ideas and opinions or the sharing of information by means of speech, writing, images (including in electronic form) or through events that we hold, including the lawful expression of ideas and opinions that some may find objectionable or offensive.
follow_up.actual_answer:
The University of Essex ensures that the core values of academic freedom and freedom of speech are promoted in academic and university life by recognizing the importance of tolerance of a wide range of ideas and opinions that are lawfully expressed (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf). 

The University also promotes an environment where intense inquiry and informed argument can generate lasting ideas, and where academic staff have freedom within the law to question and test received wisdom, and to put forward new ideas and controversial or unpopular opinions (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf).

Additionally, the University has a duty to ensure that academic staff have freedom within the law to question and test received wisdom, and to put forward new ideas and controversial or unpopular opinions, without placing themselves in jeopardy of losing their jobs or privileges (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf).

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf

primary.question: What is the frequency of review for departmental arrangements related to Professional Doctorates, and when are changes necessary?
primary.expected_answer: Departmental arrangements will be reviewed every three years or sooner if the department wishes to make any major changes to the arrangements or if any changes to University policies, procedures or regulations require it.
primary.actual_answer:
The departmental documentation and arrangements for Professional Doctorates should be reviewed every three years, or sooner if the department wishes to make any major changes to the arrangements, or if any changes to University policies, procedures, or regulations require it (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/code-practice-professional-doctorates.pdf). 

Similarly, for Trust arrangements, the review should also occur every three years or sooner under the same conditions (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/code-of-practice-professional-doctorates-tavistock.pdf).

primary.expected_answer: All University students are required to comply with the regulations of the University regarding conduct. Students enrolled on courses where a practical professional placement is required (including health, social work and education) have additional responsibilities placed upon them regarding not only their conduct but also their professional suitability.

The scope of the Fitness to Practise Procedure at the University of Essex applies to all relevant courses leading to awards of the University, particularly those that require a practical professional placement, such as health, social work, and education (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/procedures-fitness-to-practise.pdf). primary.question: What is the purpose of providing healthcare students with clinical uniform free of charge before they start their practice placements?
primary.expected_answer: To ensure that students are prepared and compliant with relevant codes of practice when starting their practice placements.
primary.actual_answer:
The purpose of providing healthcare students with clinical uniform free of charge before they start their practice placements is to ensure they meet the requirement for a professional clinical appearance while on placement, as stated in the University of Essex Professional Clinical Appearance Code of Practice (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/professional-clinical-appearance-code-of-practice.pdf).

follow_up.question: What happens if a student fails to comply with the University's Professional Clinical Appearance Code of Practice during their placement?
follow_up.expected_answer: Students who fail to comply may face disciplinary action and could be required to rectify any breaches.
follow_up.actual_answer:
If a student fails to comply with the University's Professional Clinical Appearance Code of Practice during their placement, they may be considered to be in breach of the University of Essex Code of Conduct (relating to safety on University premises) and the responsibility to follow relevant regulatory and/or professional body codes of practice. This may lead to students being referred for investigation by the Conduct Team, and referred to the Professional Suitability Group as per Fitness to Practise procedures (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/professional-clinical-appearance-code-of-practice.pdf).

primary.question: What are the roles and responsibilities of Student Representatives according to this policy?
primary.expected_answer: Student Representatives are expected to engage proactively, provide feedback through SVGs and mid-term module feedback, and be part of the first tier of the representation system.
primary.actual_answer:
According to the Student Voice Policy (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/academic-standards-and-quality/student-voice-policy.pdf), the roles and responsibilities of Student Representatives include:

* Attending mandatory training to ensure they are prepared for the role
* Participating in meetings and other related activities
* Gathering and providing student feedback
* Attending all meetings expected of their role, either in person or remotely
* Notifying key contacts and arranging for another Student Rep to take their place if they are unable to attend a meeting
* Contributing to discussions and decision-making processes
* Acting in accordance with the Code of Conduct and guiding principles
* Being accountable to the students they represent and the Students' Union as a whole.

Sources: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/academic-standards-and-quality/student-voice-policy.pdf

* Elected Officers (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/academic-standards-and-quality/student-voice-policy.pdf)

primary.question: What are the conditions under which a student can apply for academic flexibility according to the policy?
primary.expected_answer: Students must be dual-career athletes, either receiving a University of Essex Sports Scholarship or being TASS-funded athlete students. Applications require evidence that the student is training or competing at regional or national level.
primary.actual_answer: A student can apply for academic flexibility if they are a dual-career athlete who has been selected or possibly selected for national training camps, international competition, or representation of University of Essex Rebels teams in professional or British Universities and Colleges Sport (BUCS) competitions that they feel will interfere with their academic study. They must submit an Application for Academic Flexibility Form with supporting evidence to the University's Sports Performance Team no later than six weeks after the start of the academic year (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-flexibility-policy-for-taught-students-sport.pdf).

primary.question: What are some risks that could affect the delivery of education at University of Essex according to the Student Protection Plan?
primary.expected_answer: The University has identified several risks that could result in a failure to provide students with a fully transformative educational experience, including financial insolvency, course closure, IT infrastructure issues, staff shortages, building capacity limitations, and loss of qualifications or international student teaching rights.
primary.actual_answer:
According to the Student Protection Plan (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/ofs/student-protection-plan.pdf), some risks that could affect the delivery of education at University of Essex include:
1. The University is no longer financially sustainable
2. Changes to or discontinuation of a course
3. Closure of the Department in which a course is being offered
4. Closure of the location or campus at which a course is being taught
5. Insufficient IT infrastructure for course delivery
6. Lack of staff to teach a course
7. Inability of buildings or campuses to accommodate teaching events
8. Loss of the right to offer a qualification
9. Loss of the right to teach international students
10. Poor risk management at the University.

Sources: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/ofs/student-protection-plan.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are some risks that could affect the delivery of education at University of Essex according to the Student Protection Plan?

judge.justification: The assistant's answer is mostly correct but includes an additional point about poor risk management which was not in the reference answer.

follow_up.expected_answer: The University has robust financial plans and operates within a clear financial framework to ensure sustainability. The application of this framework, along with regular performance monitoring by the University Council, Policy and Resources Committee, and Audit and Risk Management Committee, helps maintain financial health into the future.

primary.question: What are the five principles that underpin the Student Engagement Policy at the University of Essex?
primary.expected_answer: The policy is underpinned by five principles: an approach to monitoring and responding to student engagement that is driven, and seen to be driven, by support and advice to succeed; visibility and a shared understanding across the institution of student engagement and the impact of our interventions; personalised interventions and mutually agreed solutions for students as far as possible, tailored to individual needs and circumstances to support students to be active participants in their education and academic progress; parity of experience for all our students regardless of academic discipline or Department through the establishment of clear institutional expectations both of the University and of our students in managing student engagement; resource deployment that focuses on effectiveness, efficiency and is targeted in support of student success.

The other three principles are not mentioned in the given context.

primary.question: What does the Fitness to Study policy address in terms of student behavior?
primary.expected_answer: The Fitness to Study policy addresses concerns over students' health or mental health difficulties that impact their studies and life at university, leading to problematic behavior or disruption to other students.
primary.actual_answer:
The Fitness to Study policy addresses student behavior that is causing concern, including behavior that is impacting adversely on others, behavior that presents a risk to themselves or others, and behavior that is disrupting the teaching, learning, or support of other students (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/fitness-to-study.pdf).

follow_up.actual_answer:
The policy ensures fairness by treating students "fairly, sensitively and with respect" and offering them "support and guidance" to help them engage with their studies (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/fitness-to-study.pdf). primary.question: What types of prior learning can be recognized under the RPL policy at the University of Essex?
primary.expected_answer: Prior learning can include previous formal study, work experience, community or voluntary experience, and existing awards from the University of Essex or its partner institutions.
primary.actual_answer:
According to the RPL policy (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/academic-standards-and-quality/rpl_policy.pdf), the types of prior learning that can be recognized include: 
- credit undertaken at another university 
- prior learning through professional experience 
- existing University of Essex award towards another award (re-use of credit) 
- prior training and/or research.

These forms of prior learning can be recognized for academic purposes, either through credit or other formal recognition. 

Sources: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/academic-standards-and-quality/rpl_policy.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: What types of prior learning can be recognized under the RPL policy at the University of Essex?

judge.justification: The answer is mostly correct but lacks mention of community or voluntary experience.

follow_up.question: Can you explain how prior learning is assigned a credit value for students on degree apprenticeship courses?
follow_up.expected_answer: Prior learning can be assigned a credit value to allow the student not to take specific modules that would normally be included on their course, particularly important as their training plan should ensure they do not duplicate knowledge, skills and behaviours they already have within their apprenticeship programme.
follow_up.actual_answer:
For students on degree apprenticeship courses, prior learning (for example, through professional experience) is assigned a credit value to allow the student not to take specific modules that would normally be included on their course. This is done to ensure that they do not duplicate knowledge, skills, and behaviours they already have within their apprenticeship programme. 

Sources: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/academic-standards-and-quality/rpl_policy.pdf

primary.question: What are the responsibilities of the Academic Registrar in managing fee liability for students?
primary.expected_answer: The Academic Registrar or their nominee has responsibility for managing the processes associated with fee liability for students and the financial responsibility for students who may withdraw or intermit.
primary.actual_answer:
The Academic Registrar or their nominee has responsibility for managing the processes associated with fee liability for students and the financial responsibility for students who may withdraw or intermit (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/tuition-fee-payment-and-liability-policy-2025-26.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/payment-liability-policy.pdf). 

They are also responsible on behalf of the University Steering Group for oversight and regular review of the Tuition Fee Payment and Liability Policy and its effective implementation and dissemination to staff and students. 

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/tuition-fee-payment-and-liability-policy-2025-26.pdf
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/payment-liability-policy.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are the responsibilities of the Academic Registrar in managing fee liability for students?

judge.justification: The answer is mostly correct but includes additional responsibilities not explicitly stated in the reference answer.

follow_up.question: What happens if a student withdraws from their course during the academic year according to this policy?
follow_up.expected_answer: Fee liability following withdrawal is detailed in section Fee liability following withdrawal of the document.
follow_up.actual_answer:
According to the policy (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/tuition-fee-payment-and-liability-policy-2025-26.pdf), if a student withdraws from their course, their fee liability depends on when they withdraw. 

- If a student withdraws prior to the course start date, there is no fee liability (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/tuition-fee-payment-and-liability-policy-2025-26.pdf).
- If a student withdraws within 28 days of the course start date, they are liable for 10% of the annual fee, with some exceptions (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/tuition-fee-payment-and-liability-policy-2025-26.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/payment-liability-policy.pdf).
- If a student withdraws after 28 days from the start of their course, they are liable for fees as set out in Table 3 (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/tuition-fee-payment-and-liability-policy-2025-26.pdf) or Table 5 (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/payment-liability-policy.pdf).

Sources: 
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/tuition-fee-payment-and-liability-policy-2025-26.pdf
https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/fees-and-funding/payment-liability-policy.pdf

retrieval.retrieval_query: What happens if a student withdraws from their course during the academic year according to the Tuition Fee Payment and Liability Policy?

judge.justification: The answer provides detailed information but goes beyond the reference by specifying exact percentages and timelines not directly stated in the reference.

primary.question: What does the term 'Capped Mark' refer to in the University of Essex Undergraduate Rules of Assessment?
primary.expected_answer: A capped mark is a maximum mark that can be awarded for an assessment, and if achieved, no further marks can be gained from re-assessments or supplementary examinations.
primary.actual_answer:
The term 'Capped Mark' in the University of Essex Undergraduate Rules of Assessment refers to a maximum mark that can be achieved for a module, typically set at the pass mark (40 for most undergraduate modules, 50 for modules at Levels 7 and above). This means that even if a student scores higher than the capped mark in a reassessment, their official mark will be limited to the capped mark. (Sources: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/foundation-year/roa-ug-foundation-year-rules.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/specific-courses/roa-ug-incoming-essex-abroad-rules.pdf)

_Note: this answer is based primarily on "roa-ug-foundation-year-rules.pdf". Your question could also relate to other documents (rules often differ by programme, department, or academic year) - tell me which programme or year you mean if this isn't the right one._

retrieval.retrieval_query: What does the term 'Capped Mark' refer to in the University of Essex Undergraduate Rules of Assessment?

judge.justification: The answer is mostly correct but specifies a pass mark threshold that is not mentioned in the reference answer.

follow_up.question: Can the capped mark be exceeded by a student's performance in any circumstances?
follow_up.expected_answer: No, the capped mark is the highest mark that can be achieved and cannot be exceeded through re-assessment or supplementary examination.
follow_up.actual_answer:
No, the capped mark cannot be exceeded by a student's performance in any circumstances. According to the rules of assessment (https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/pgt/2025-26/department-specific/pg_dip_periodontology_25.pdf, https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/specific-courses/roa-ug-ehs-hons-stage-two-final-rules.pdf, and others), if the pre-resubmission module aggregate was above the pass mark, the resubmission cannot improve the module aggregate mark, and it is capped at the pre-resubmission aggregate. If the pre-resubmission module aggregate was below the pass mark, the resubmission can improve the module aggregate mark, but only up to the pass mark.

https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/specific-courses/roa-ug-ehs-final-year-variations.pdf
